2: Environmental impacts of farms depend to a large extent on farmer production practices. However, the link between farmer practices and impacts is indirect (Fig. 1), as other factors (soil characteristics, weather) affect the causal chain linking farmer practices to emissions of pollutants, while the extent to which pollutant emissions cause impacts depends on the fate of the pollutant and on the sensitivity of the environmental target (Basset-Mens et al., 2006).
Methods for the environmental evaluation of farms can rely on indicators of: (i) farmer practices (means-based indicators); (ii) the

state of the farming system; (iii) emissions to the environment; or (iv) environmental impacts (effect-based indicators) (Fig. 1). Means-based indicators are easy to implement, but do not allow an actual evaluation of environmental impact (Hansen, 1996). Effect- based indicators are preferable for impact evaluation, but they are more demanding with respect to data collection and modelling of the causal chain linking farmer practices to impacts. Thus evalua- tion methods using effect-based indicators may be difficult to conduct because they are complicated, time-consuming or require data that are not available (van der Werf and Petit, 2002). The development of an operational method for the environ- mental evaluation of farms using effect-based indicators is one of the major challenges facing those working in the field of environ- mental analysis of farming systems (van der Werf and Petit, 2002). We developed such a method, based on the life cycle assessment (LCA) conceptual framework. The method was implemented in a tool for the evaluation of the sustainability of farms (Evaluation de

la Durabilité des ExploitatioNs, EDEN), in which LCA indicators are complemented by indicators of social and economic performance. This paper describes the environmental evaluation component of EDEN, which will be designated here as EDEN-E to distinguish it from the full EDEN tool. EDEN-E requires a modest amount of data readily available on-farm and thus can be used, for example, by extension personnel for routine evaluation of individual farms and by researchers for the evaluation of a large number of farms, as in land use studies (Payraudeau and van der Werf, 2005).

LCA is a compilation and evaluation of the inputs and outputs and of the potential environmental impacts of a product system (a set of unit processes connected by material, energy, product, waste or service flows, performing one or more defined functions; Guinée et al., 2002). A farm can be seen as a product system. In the Life Cycle Inventory phase the environmental interventions (i.e. resource extractions, pollutant emissions and land use) are listed. In the Life Cycle Impact Assessment phase, environmental interven- tions are interpreted in terms of environmental impacts, by multiplying each of them with a characterisation factor for each impact category to which they may contribute.

Traditionally LCA is a site- and time-independent tool that does not consider where and when environmental interventions take place (Finnveden and Nilsson, 2005). Characterisation factors typically lack site-dependent information, resulting in a site- generic impact assessment. This largely is due to practical reasons, as it is difficult to gather site-specific information for all sites included in an LCA. We introduced site-dependence in EDEN-E by distinguishing "direct" (on the farm site) environmental interven- tions from "indirect" (associated with production and supply of inputs used on the farm) environmental interventions. The method thus distinguishes a direct and an indirect component for each impact considered. This site-dependence was introduced for two reasons. First, distinguishing direct impacts from indirect impacts is useful when the evaluation method is used to find ways to decrease a farm's impacts, as it will reveal the relative importance of the two

components for each impact; consequently, it will help identify improvement strategies. Second, distinguishing direct and indirect environmental interventions will allow the use of site-specific characterisation factors, in particular for direct environmental interventions contributing to regional impacts (e.g. eutrophication, land occupation).
The objective of this paper is to present EDEN-E, describe its methodology and analyse a case study of its application to dairy farms in western France.

EDEN-E requires a modest amount of data readily available on-farm and thus can be used, for example, by extension personnel for routine evaluation of individual farms and by researchers for the evaluation of a large number of farms, as in land use studies (Payraudeau and van der Werf, 2005).

LCA is a compilation and evaluation of the inputs and outputs and of the potential environmental impacts of a product system (a set of unit processes connected by material, energy, product, waste or service flows, performing one or more defined functions; Guinée et al., 2002). A farm can be seen as a product system. In the Life Cycle Inventory phase the environmental interventions (i.e. resource extractions, pollutant emissions and land use) are listed. In the Life Cycle Impact Assessment phase, environmental interven- tions are interpreted in terms of environmental impacts, by multiplying each of them with a characterisation factor for each impact category to which they may contribute.

Traditionally LCA is a site- and time-independent tool that does not consider where and when environmental interventions take place (Finnveden and Nilsson, 2005). Characterisation factors typically lack site-dependent information, resulting in a site- generic impact assessment. This largely is due to practical reasons, as it is difficult to gather site-specific information for all sites included in an LCA. We introduced site-dependence in EDEN-E by distinguishing "direct" (on the farm site) environmental interven- tions from "indirect" (associated with production and supply of inputs used on the farm) environmental interventions. The method thus distinguishes a direct and an indirect component for each impact considered. This site-dependence was introduced for two reasons. First, distinguishing direct impacts from indirect impacts is useful when the evaluation method is used to find ways to decrease a farm's impacts, as it will reveal the relative importance of the two

components for each impact; consequently, it will help identify improvement strategies. Second, distinguishing direct and indirect environmental interventions will allow the use of site-specific characterisation factors, in particular for direct environmental interventions contributing to regional impacts (e.g. eutrophication, land occupation).
The objective of this paper is to present EDEN-E, describe its methodology and analyse a case study of its application to dairy farms in western France.
8: The goals of EDEN-E are to: (i) analyse the environmental impacts of a farm according to the LCA approach; (ii) determine the contribution of the farm's components and environmental inter- ventions to its impacts; and (iii) support decision making by eval- uating the environmental consequences of modifications to the farm's management or structure.

The intended users of the method are agricultural advisors and researchers. EDEN-E was developed within the project "Evaluation of the sustainability of dairy farming systems in Bretagne" (Kanyarushoki et al., 2007), carried out from 2003 to 2006 by Agrotransfert Bretagne, a structure which formalises collaborative projects between the French National Institute for Agronomic Research (INRA) and the Bretagne Chamber of Agriculture (CAB). Agrotransfert projects use INRA research results to develop oper- ational tools for agricultural and rural management. The project was directed by a team consisting of a project manager and one representative each from INRA and CAB. A steering committee, consisting of agricultural advisors from CAB, experts of institutes for applied research and farmer organisations, four dairy farmers and the project team, met twice a year to oversee the project. In its

present version EDEN-E is implemented for farms combining field crops, pastures and dairy cows in Bretagne (western France). EDEN-E applies a cradle-to-farm-gate analysis, meaning that the farming system evaluated consists of the farm and its main inputs, but that products are no longer part of the system once they leave the farm (Fig. 2). Some of the farms import manure and sewage sludge; production and transport of these inputs, supplied at no cost, are considered to be part of the system of their supplier and therefore not included in the system here; however, emissions following their application on the farm are included. Non-agri- cultural parts of the farm such as the farmer's house, woodlands and forests are not included in the system, nor are construction of farm buildings, farm roads and drainage networks. The use of disinfectants, detergents, antibiotics, hormones and other medi- cation was not considered, due to lack of data concerning the production and environmental fate of these inputs. For pesticides, non-renewable energy use for production and supply was considered, but impacts associated with the use of pesticides (toxic effects) were not considered, due to lack of appropriate characterisation factors. This is regrettable; however, compared to other agricultural sectors (arable crops, fruit, vegetables), dairy farms use fewer pesticides. Temporal coverage of EDEN-E is a period of 1 year, usually corresponding to the period used in a farm's bookkeeping. The mode of LCA analysis is descriptive (attributional) (Guinée et al., 2002, p. 462), which is appropriate as the primary objective of EDEN-E is to document past activities (Frischknecht, 2006). A farm performs several functions: producing food, providing environ- mental services (e.g. maintaining water quality), and providing income for the farmer. According to these functions, EDEN-E uses three functional units: 10001 of milk sold, 1 ha of land occupied, and 1000 euros of gross farm income. Impacts per 1000 of milk are converted to 1000 kg of fat-and-protein-corrected milk (FPCM, Thomassen and de Boer, 2005) for this article.

10: We used two complementary approaches to establish a farm's environmental inventory. Many environmental interventions are linearly related to the amounts of inputs used. This is the case for all indirect interventions (e.g. off-farm nitrate loss depends on the amount and types of plant-based inputs such as concentrated feed)

and for some direct interventions on-farm ammonia emissions following application of ammonium nitrate fertiliser depend on the amount used). EDEN-E contains an inventory table listing envi- ronmental interventions for the following input types:

<list>Energy carriers (e.g. diesel, natural gas, electricity, lubricants) 
Pesticides 
Plastic sheeting 
Agricultural machines 
Operations carried out by agricultural contractors (e.g. ploughing, ensiling a crop) 
Mineral fertilisers (e.g. nitrogen (N), phosphorus (P), potassium (K), lime) 
Concentrated feed (including cereals, pulses, seed cake, dried lucerne) 
Fodder and stable bedding (e.g. hay, silage, straw) 
Animals</list>

For all of these inputs the amount used for the year under investigation is taken directly from the farm's records, except for agricultural machines, which have a lifetime exceeding 1 year. For machines, allocation is performed according to the hours (or hectares) of use within the year under consideration compared to the total hours (or hectares) of use over the life of the machine (Audsley et al., 1997). Data on the environmental interventions associated with the production and supply of the inputs contained in this inventory table are based on various publicly available sources such as BUWAL (1996), Gaillard et al. (1997), Patyk and Reinhardt (1997), Davis and Haglund (1999), and on unpublished data from INRA at Rennes. Most on-farm emissions of pollutants are not linearly related to amounts of farm inputs used. For the establishment of an inventory of on-farm emissions of pollutants two approaches can be found (Payraudeau et al., 2006). The first approach consists of estimating the emissions at the level of the farm's components (i.e. its indi- vidual fields, buildings, manure storage facilities) and then to combine the results at the whole-farm scale. The advantage of this method is that it works with the basic units on a farm; its main inconvenience is the time needed to collect information for each individual component. Many published examples of this approach exist (e.g. Berntsen et al., 2003; Keating et al., 2003; Rotz et al., 2007). To limit the time required for data collection we followed
Fig. 2. Flow diagram and system boundary for a dairy farm in EDEN-E.
a second approach that estimates emissions as much as possible directly at the farm scale, based largely on farm-gate balances (Kristensen et al., 2005).

Emissions of N compounds were estimated as proposed by Payraudeau et al. (2006), except for calculations of emissions of nitrous oxide (N20), which were updated according to recent IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). In brief, this approach first determines the surplus of a farm-gate N balance. Inputs considered are mineral fertiliser, imported manure and sewage sludge, symbiotic N fixa- tion, atmospheric N deposition, plant-based products (e.g. fodder, concentrated feed, straw) and livestock; outputs are exported manure, plant-based products, milk and livestock. The surplus of the farm-gate N balance then is adjusted for changes in on-farm stocks of mineral fertiliser, plant-based products and livestock. This means for example that an increase of 1000 kg in the stock of N in fertiliser over the year considered will be booked as an output of 1000 kg of N. Next, emissions of ammonia (NH3), nitric oxide (NO), N20 and dinitrogen (N2) are estimated using emission factors (EF). Gaseous emissions at the farm level depend on numerous factors, including the quantity of N excreted by livestock, the site of excretion (on pasture, in a building), type of manure (liquid, solid), storage conditions, and season and technology of field application of manure. EDEN-E uses a method proposed by CORPEN (1999) for calculating excreted N for dairy cows and heifers (CORPEN, 2001) according to the type of ration and the level of milk production. Time spent at building and pasture is used to allocate excreted N between the two. After subtraction of gaseous losses from the building and during storage, manure N available for field spreading can be calculated. Emissions following spreading of manure are calculated considering season, vegetation cover and application technique. Estimation of gaseous N emissions thus requires data on animal numbers, feed rations, milk production level, time spent in building and pasture, type of manure (all at the farm level), season, vegetation cover, and technology of manure application (at the level of individual fields).

Nitrate (NO3) losses at the farm scale were estimated as the difference between the farm-gate N balance and the gaseous N emissions. This approach has the inconvenience of accumulating the uncertainties of previous calculations into the NO3 leaching estimate. The feasibility of the method and the robustness of the NO3 leaching estimate were examined in a study of 24 farms in a small (12 km ² catchment area. Groundwater NO3 concentrations as calculated from the NO3 emissions estimated with our approach (171 mg of NO3/l) were found to be reasonably similar to NO3 concentrations measured in the upper groundwater of the catch- ment area (129-149 mg/l) (Payraudeau et al., 2006), providing a first validation of the robustness of the method. A Monte Carlo approach was used to evaluate the uncertainty of estimated gaseous N emissions and NO3 losses, revealing reasonably small confidence intervals for these estimates (Payraudeau et al., 2007).

Run-off of phosphorus as phosphate (PO4-P) to surface water was estimated according to Rossier (1998): an EF of 0.01 kg of PO4-P per kg P input from organic and mineral fertiliser was used. For cadmium (Cd), copper (Cu), nickel (Ni), lead (Pb) and zinc (Zn) a farm-gate balance was established, considering input by mineral and organic fertiliser, plant-based products and livestock; and output via manure, plant-based products, animals and milk. The surplus of this balance was considered to be an emission to the soil. Heavy-metal content values used for farm inputs and outputs are detailed in Kanyarushoki et al. (2007).

Methane (CH4) emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated according to the Tier 2 method proposed by IPCC (2006). For each animal subcategory this method requires data on animal weight, weight gain per day, feeding situation, milk fat content, percentage of females giving birth and feed digestibility. Data

corresponding to the situation of dairy farms in Bretagne were supplied by P. Faverdin (pers. comm., 2007). Thus annual values for enteric CH4 emissions were obtained for heifers according to their age years, 1-2 years, 2-3 years) and for cows according to their level of milk production.

CH4 emissions from manure management were calculated according to the Tier 2 method proposed by IPCC (2006). This yields values for CH4 emissions that depend on animal subcategory (cows, and heifers according to their age) and the manure management system.

The product system evaluated (the farm, from cradle-to-farm- gate) contains several unit processes yielding more than one product (e.g. many ingredients for concentrated feed are co-prod- ucts (soy cake, wheat gluten)); for these processes environmental interventions were allocated according to the economic value of the co-products.

For the functional unit "1000 kg of FPCM," a first version of EDEN-E allocated impacts among milk, animals (cull cows, calves), and crop products according to the proportion of total revenue the farm received from sales of each product category during the analysis year. This "all-product" economic allocation method was rightly challenged by one of the reviewers of this paper, as envi- ronmental interventions associated with the animals (e.g. methane emissions) will be partially allocated to crop products and vice versa. We therefore decided to avoid allocation between animal and crop products and separate the farms in two parts: production of crop products not used for animal production and all other farm processes.

Consequently, to calculate impacts per 1000 kg of FPCM, we processed the data to remove from each farm's inventory all inputs, outputs, and farm useable agricultural area (UAA) used for crops and forages grown on the farm but not used for animal production (i.e., those sold or stockpiled). Specifically, we estimated the surface area necessary to produce the quantity of each crop or forage sold or stockpiled; subtracted any mineral or organic fertilisers, nitrogen fixation, plastics, machinery, and fuel used or occurring in this area to produce the crop or forage; and subtracted the output of N, P, K, and heavy-metals in crops and forage sold or stocked. After pro- cessing, remaining environmental interventions were due only to milk and animal production. In the final step, we used economic allocation to allocate these remaining environmental interventions between milk and animal production (82 and 80% to milk for conventional and organic production, respectively).

For the functional unit "1 ha of land occupied" the impacts of all of the farm's products are taken into account, and both land on-farm as well as the estimated area of land occupied off-farm are considered. Land on-farm consists of the farm's UAA (i.e. the total farm area minus woodlands, forests, buildings, paved surfaces), whereas land off-farm (e.g. for the production of ingredients for concentrated feed) is estimated from amounts and types of plant- based inputs (concentrated feed, fodder, stable bedding) used. While farms used a variety of plant-based inputs, we assumed that the area of land needed to produce a kilogram of each raw ingre- dient in feeds, fodder, and bedding did not differ between organic and conventional farms (i.e. identical yields, assuming sufficient N input (Pimentel et al., 2005)).

The product system evaluated (the farm, from cradle-to-farm- gate) contains several unit processes yielding more than one product (e.g. many ingredients for concentrated feed are co-prod- ucts (soy cake, wheat gluten)); for these processes environmental interventions were allocated according to the economic value of the co-products.

For the functional unit "1000 kg of FPCM," a first version of EDEN-E allocated impacts among milk, animals (cull cows, calves), and crop products according to the proportion of total revenue the farm received from sales of each product category during the analysis year. This "all-product" economic allocation method was rightly challenged by one of the reviewers of this paper, as envi- ronmental interventions associated with the animals (e.g. methane emissions) will be partially allocated to crop products and vice versa. We therefore decided to avoid allocation between animal and crop products and separate the farms in two parts: production of crop products not used for animal production and all other farm processes.

Consequently, to calculate impacts per 1000 kg of FPCM, we processed the data to remove from each farm's inventory all inputs, outputs, and farm useable agricultural area (UAA) used for crops and forages grown on the farm but not used for animal production (i.e., those sold or stockpiled). a second approach that estimates emissions as much as possible directly at the farm scale, based largely on farm-gate balances (Kristensen et al., 2005).

Emissions of N compounds were estimated as proposed by Payraudeau et al. In brief, this approach first determines the surplus of a farm-gate N balance. The surplus of the farm-gate N balance then is adjusted for changes in on-farm stocks of mineral fertiliser, plant-based products and livestock. This means for example that an increase of 1000 kg in the stock of N in fertiliser over the year considered will be booked as an output of 1000 kg of N. For all of these inputs the amount used for the year under investigation is taken directly from the farm's records, except for agricultural machines, which have a lifetime exceeding 1 year. (1997), Patyk and Reinhardt (1997), Davis and Haglund (1999), and on unpublished data from INRA at Rennes. Most on-farm emissions of pollutants are not linearly related to amounts of farm inputs used. For the establishment of an inventory of on-farm emissions of pollutants two approaches can be found (Payraudeau et al., 2006). The first approach consists of estimating the emissions at the level of the farm's components (i.e. its indi- vidual fields, buildings, manure storage facilities) and then to combine the results at the whole-farm scale. The advantage of this method is that it works with the basic units on a farm; its main inconvenience is the time needed to collect information for each individual component. Many published examples of this approach exist (e.g. Berntsen et al., 2003; Keating et al., 2003; Rotz et al., 2007). To limit the time required for data collection we followed

a second approach that estimates emissions as much as possible directly at the farm scale, based largely on farm-gate balances (Kristensen et al., 2005).

Emissions of N compounds were estimated as proposed by Payraudeau et al. (2006), except for calculations of emissions of nitrous oxide (N20), which were updated according to recent IPCC guidelines (IPCC, 2006). In brief, this approach first determines the surplus of a farm-gate N balance. Inputs considered are mineral fertiliser, imported manure and sewage sludge, symbiotic N fixa- tion, atmospheric N deposition, plant-based products (e.g. fodder, concentrated feed, straw) and livestock; outputs are exported manure, plant-based products, milk and livestock. The surplus of the farm-gate N balance then is adjusted for changes in on-farm stocks of mineral fertiliser, plant-based products and livestock. This means for example that an increase of 1000 kg in the stock of N in fertiliser over the year considered will be booked as an output of 1000 kg of N. Next, emissions of ammonia (NH3), nitric oxide (NO), N20 and dinitrogen (N2) are estimated using emission factors (EF). Gaseous emissions at the farm level depend on numerous factors, including the quantity of N excreted by livestock, the site of excretion (on pasture, in a building), type of manure (liquid, solid), storage conditions, and season and technology of field application of manure. EDEN-E uses a method proposed by CORPEN (1999) for calculating excreted N for dairy cows and heifers (CORPEN, 2001) according to the type of ration and the level of milk production. Time spent at building and pasture is used to allocate excreted N between the two. After subtraction of gaseous losses from the building and during storage, manure N available for field spreading can be calculated. Emissions following spreading of manure are calculated considering season, vegetation cover and application technique. Estimation of gaseous N emissions thus requires data on animal numbers, feed rations, milk production level, time spent in building and pasture, type of manure (all at the farm level), season, vegetation cover, and technology of manure application (at the level of individual fields).

Nitrate (NO3) losses at the farm scale were estimated as the difference between the farm-gate N balance and the gaseous N emissions. This approach has the inconvenience of accumulating the uncertainties of previous calculations into the NO3 leaching estimate. The feasibility of the method and the robustness of the NO3 leaching estimate were examined in a study of 24 farms in a small (12 km ² catchment area. Groundwater NO3 concentrations as calculated from the NO3 emissions estimated with our approach (171 mg of NO3/l) were found to be reasonably similar to NO3 concentrations measured in the upper groundwater of the catch- ment area (129-149 mg/l) (Payraudeau et al., 2006), providing a first validation of the robustness of the method. A Monte Carlo approach was used to evaluate the uncertainty of estimated gaseous N emissions and NO3 losses, revealing reasonably small confidence intervals for these estimates (Payraudeau et al., 2007).

Run-off of phosphorus as phosphate (PO4-P) to surface water was estimated according to Rossier (1998): an EF of 0.01 kg of PO4-P per kg P input from organic and mineral fertiliser was used. For cadmium (Cd), copper (Cu), nickel (Ni), lead (Pb) and zinc (Zn) a farm-gate balance was established, considering input by mineral and organic fertiliser, plant-based products and livestock; and output via manure, plant-based products, animals and milk. The surplus of this balance was considered to be an emission to the soil. Heavy-metal content values used for farm inputs and outputs are detailed in Kanyarushoki et al. (2007).

Methane (CH4) emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated according to the Tier 2 method proposed by IPCC (2006). For each animal subcategory this method requires data on animal weight, weight gain per day, feeding situation, milk fat content, percentage of females giving birth and feed digestibility. Data

corresponding to the situation of dairy farms in Bretagne were supplied by P. Faverdin (pers. comm., 2007). Thus annual values for enteric CH4 emissions were obtained for heifers according to their age years, 1-2 years, 2-3 years) and for cows according to their level of milk production.

CH4 emissions from manure management were calculated according to the Tier 2 method proposed by IPCC (2006). This yields values for CH4 emissions that depend on animal subcategory (cows, and heifers according to their age) and the manure management system.

The product system evaluated (the farm, from cradle-to-farm- gate) contains several unit processes yielding more than one product (e.g. many ingredients for concentrated feed are co-prod- ucts (soy cake, wheat gluten)); for these processes environmental interventions were allocated according to the economic value of the co-products.

For the functional unit "1000 kg of FPCM," a first version of EDEN-E allocated impacts among milk, animals (cull cows, calves), and crop products according to the proportion of total revenue the farm received from sales of each product category during the analysis year. This "all-product" economic allocation method was rightly challenged by one of the reviewers of this paper, as envi- ronmental interventions associated with the animals (e.g. methane emissions) will be partially allocated to crop products and vice versa. We therefore decided to avoid allocation between animal and crop products and separate the farms in two parts: production of crop products not used for animal production and all other farm processes.

Consequently, to calculate impacts per 1000 kg of FPCM, we processed the data to remove from each farm's inventory all inputs, outputs, and farm useable agricultural area (UAA) used for crops and forages grown on the farm but not used for animal production (i.e., those sold or stockpiled). Specifically, we estimated the surface area necessary to produce the quantity of each crop or forage sold or stockpiled; subtracted any mineral or organic fertilisers, nitrogen fixation, plastics, machinery, and fuel used or occurring in this area to produce the crop or forage; and subtracted the output of N, P, K, and heavy-metals in crops and forage sold or stocked. After pro- cessing, remaining environmental interventions were due only to milk and animal production. In the final step, we used economic allocation to allocate these remaining environmental interventions between milk and animal production (82 and 80% to milk for conventional and organic production, respectively).

For the functional unit "1 ha of land occupied" the impacts of all of the farm's products are taken into account, and both land on-farm as well as the estimated area of land occupied off-farm are considered. Land on-farm consists of the farm's UAA (i.e. the total farm area minus woodlands, forests, buildings, paved surfaces), whereas land off-farm (e.g. for the production of ingredients for concentrated feed) is estimated from amounts and types of plant- based inputs (concentrated feed, fodder, stable bedding) used. While farms used a variety of plant-based inputs, we assumed that the area of land needed to produce a kilogram of each raw ingre- dient in feeds, fodder, and bedding did not differ between organic and conventional farms (i.e. identical yields, assuming sufficient N input (Pimentel et al., 2005)).

For cadmium (Cd), copper (Cu), nickel (Ni), lead (Pb) and zinc (Zn) a farm-gate balance was established, considering input by mineral and organic fertiliser, plant-based products and livestock; and output via manure, plant-based products, animals and milk. The surplus of this balance was considered to be an emission to the soil. Heavy-metal content values used for farm inputs and outputs are detailed in Kanyarushoki et al. (2007).

Methane (CH4) emissions from enteric fermentation were calculated according to the Tier 2 method proposed by IPCC (2006). For each animal subcategory this method requires data on animal weight, weight gain per day, feeding situation, milk fat content, percentage of females giving birth and feed digestibility. Data

corresponding to the situation of dairy farms in Bretagne were supplied by P. Faverdin (pers. comm., 2007). Thus annual values for enteric CH4 emissions were obtained for heifers according to their age years, 1-2 years, 2-3 years) and for cows according to their level of milk production.

CH4 emissions from manure management were calculated according to the Tier 2 method proposed by IPCC (2006). This yields values for CH4 emissions that depend on animal subcategory (cows, and heifers according to their age) and the manure management system.

The product system evaluated (the farm, from cradle-to-farm- gate) contains several unit processes yielding more than one product (e.g. many ingredients for concentrated feed are co-prod- ucts (soy cake, wheat gluten)); for these processes environmental interventions were allocated according to the economic value of the co-products.

For the functional unit "1000 kg of FPCM," a first version of EDEN-E allocated impacts among milk, animals (cull cows, calves), and crop products according to the proportion of total revenue the farm received from sales of each product category during the analysis year. This "all-product" economic allocation method was rightly challenged by one of the reviewers of this paper, as envi- ronmental interventions associated with the animals (e.g. methane emissions) will be partially allocated to crop products and vice versa. We therefore decided to avoid allocation between animal and crop products and separate the farms in two parts: production of crop products not used for animal production and all other farm processes.

Consequently, to calculate impacts per 1000 kg of FPCM, we processed the data to remove from each farm's inventory all inputs, outputs, and farm useable agricultural area (UAA) used for crops and forages grown on the farm but not used for animal production (i.e., those sold or stockpiled). Specifically, we estimated the surface area necessary to produce the quantity of each crop or forage sold or stockpiled; subtracted any mineral or organic fertilisers, nitrogen fixation, plastics, machinery, and fuel used or occurring in this area to produce the crop or forage; and subtracted the output of N, P, K, and heavy-metals in crops and forage sold or stocked. After pro- cessing, remaining environmental interventions were due only to milk and animal production. In the final step, we used economic allocation to allocate these remaining environmental interventions between milk and animal production (82 and 80% to milk for conventional and organic production, respectively).

For the functional unit "1 ha of land occupied" the impacts of all of the farm's products are taken into account, and both land on-farm as well as the estimated area of land occupied off-farm are considered. Land on-farm consists of the farm's UAA (i.e. the total farm area minus woodlands, forests, buildings, paved surfaces), whereas land off-farm (e.g. for the production of ingredients for concentrated feed) is estimated from amounts and types of plant- based inputs (concentrated feed, fodder, stable bedding) used. While farms used a variety of plant-based inputs, we assumed that the area of land needed to produce a kilogram of each raw ingre- dient in feeds, fodder, and bedding did not differ between organic and conventional farms (i.e. identical yields, assuming sufficient N input (Pimentel et al., 2005)).
20: The following impact categories and characterisation methods are implemented in EDEN-E. Eutrophication covers all potential impacts of high environmental levels of macronutrients, in partic- ular N and P. As recommended by Guinée et al. (2002), eutrophi- cation potential (EP) was calculated using the generic EP factors in kg PO4-equivalents., NH3: 0.35, NO3: 0.10, NO: 0.20, NO2: 0.13, NOx:

0.13, PO4: 1. Acidifying pollutants have a wide variety of impacts on soil, groundwater, surface waters, biological organisms, ecosystems and materials (buildings). As recommended by Guinée et al. (2002), acidification potential (AP) was calculated using the average European AP factors by Huijbregts (1999a) in kg SO2-equivalents, NH3: 1.6, NO2: 0.5, NOx: 0.5, SO2: 1.2. Terrestrial ecotoxicity refers to impacts of toxic substances on terrestrial ecosystems. As recom- mended by Guinée et al. (2002), terrestrial ecotoxicity potential (TETP) was calculated using the TETP factors for infinite time horizon and global scale by Huijbregts (1999b) in kg 1,4-dichloro- benzene-equivalents (DCB-eq.), Cd: 170, Cu: 14, Ni: 240, Pb: 33, Zn: 25.

Climate change is defined here as the impact of emissions on the heat radiation absorption of the atmosphere. As recommended by Guinée et al. (2002), global warming potential for a 100-year time horizon (GWP100) was calculated according to the most recent GWP100 factors from the IPCC (Forster et al., 2007) in kg CO2- equivalents, CO2: 1, N20: 298, CH4: 25. Non-renewable energy use refers to the depletion of non-renewable energetic resources. Non- renewable energy use was calculated using lower heating values proposed in the cumulative energy demand method in the software SimaPro 7.1.8 (PRé Consultants, 2004), crude oil: 42.6 MJ/kg, natural gas: 35 MJ/m³, uranium: 451,000 MJ/kg, coal: 18 MJ/kg, lignite: 8 MJ/kg, natural gas from oil production: 9 MJ/m³ Land occu- pation refers to the loss of land as a resource, in the sense of being temporarily unavailable for other purposes due to the growing of crops. This is a quantitative assessment, which does not distinguish quality of land use. Pesticide use is quantified as kg of active ingredient used for crop production.

22: EDEN-E was used for the environmental evaluation of a group of 47 dairy farms, consisting of 41 conventional farms and six organic farms. These farms represent a subset of the 60 farms evaluated in Kanyarushoki et al. (2008), for which sufficient data were available to separate environmental interventions associated with the production of crop products for sale from those associated with other farm processes. The conventional farms belong to the Equi- libre Travail Revenu Environnement (ETRE) dairy farm network, which consists of 65 farms in Bretagne selected for their good economic performance. The farms in the ETRE network are moni- tored by Chambres d'Agriculture de Bretagne (CAB) with respect to their economic performance, farmer working hours and conditions, and their environmental impacts. The objective of the network is to analyse and characterise how these farms function and how they are managed, in order to draw lessons for economically less effi- cient dairy farms in Bretagne. The organic farms belong to the "BIO" dairy farm network, consisting of 20 organic farms in Bretagne selected for their good economic performance. The objective of the BIO network is similar to that of the ETRE network (pers. comm., F. Roger, 2007). Given the characteristics of the ETRE and BIO networks, the farms analysed clearly should not be considered to represent the population of organic and conventional farms in Bretagne, but rather a subset of these populations with above- average economic performance.

We evaluated these farms using EDEN for two main reasons: to test the tool and to establish benchmark values for its impact indicators. Data were collected in 2006 by extension personnel of the CAB; for each farm this concerned a 1-year period (a fiscal year) between January 2003 and December 2005. This operation revealed that the tool's data collection module was sufficiently versatile to accommodate a wide variety of situations encountered on the farms with respect to farm input and output items encountered, housing and manure management systems, techniques used for

manure application, crops grown, grazing and feeding strategies. Three-to-four hours of on-farm data collection were sufficient, provided that source documents such as accounting books and record books on fertilisation and milk production were available. To evaluate the results, we performed Student's t-tests for inde- pendent samples to compare group means, assuming significant differences at p < 0.05.

Organic and conventional farms examined in this study had significant (p < 0.05) differences in mean values for certain farm- structure, input-use and output-level data (Table 1). Compared to conventional farms, the organic farms had a smaller percentage of fodder crops and grass area that consisted of silage maize (6 VS 30%) and pastured cows more days per year (244 VS 198). Input-use was higher for conventional than for organic farms: more concentrated feed per cow (761 VS 207 kg), more pesticide active ingredient (777 VS g ha-1), and more total N input (152 VS 73 kg Mean annual FPCM production per cow was 5507 kg for organic and 7678 kg for conventional farms; the latter is well above the corre- sponding average for dairy farms in Bretagne (6330 kg, Agreste Bretagne, 2007). FPCM production per ha of on-farm fodder crops and grass was larger for conventional than for organic farms (7197 VS 4416 kg). The surplus of the N farm-gate balance (N inputs-N outputs) was larger for the conventional than for the organic farms (88 vs 38 kg Only one farm of each type exported organic fertiliser (i.e. manure).

26: Expressed per 1000 kg of FPCM sold, mean emissions were similar for the two systems for CO2, N20, NH3, NOx, NO3, Cu, Pb and Zn. In contrast, conventional farms had significantly higher emis- sions for NO, SO2, PO4, Cd and Ni, whereas organic farms had significantly higher emissions for CH4 (Table 2). Expressed per ha of land occupied, mean emissions of all substances were significantly higher for conventional than for organic farms except for CH4, NO3, Cu, Pb, and Zn (Table 2). Mean significant differences were partic- ularly large >100% higher) for, SO2, PO4, Cd, and Ni.
27: Expressed per 1000 kg of FPCM, mean eutrophication was not significantly different between conventional and organic farms (7.1 VS 5.0 kg PO4-eq.); for both systems it was due mainly to direct emissions (Table 3). The coefficient of variation (CV) for eutrophica- tion for conventional farms was half that of organic farms (37 VS 74%). When expressed per ha of land occupied, eutrophication was larger for conventional than for organic farms (39.8 VS 20.7 kg PO4-eq.).

Per 1000 kg of FPCM, acidification was not significantly different between conventional and organic farms (7.6 VS 6.8 kg SO2-eq.); for both systems it was due mainly to direct emissions (Table 3). Acidification CV was equal (16%) for both systems. Per ha, acidifi- cation was larger for conventional than for organic farms (48.1 VS 31.0 kg SO2-eq.).

Per 1000 kg of FPCM, climate change was not significantly different between conventional and organic farms (1037 vs 1082 kg CO2-eq.); for both it was due mainly to direct impacts (Table 3). Climate change CV was smaller for conventional than for organic farms (12 VS 14%). Per ha, climate change was larger for conven- tional than for organic farms (6271 vs 4887 kg CO2-eq.).

41: LCA studies of cow-milk production from Germany (Haas et al., 2001), Sweden (Cederberg and Flysjö, 2004), the Netherlands (Thomassen et al., 2008), and New Zealand (Basset-Mens et al., 2009) have been published since 2001 (Fig. 5). However, methods of calculation used (e.g. for establishing the environmental inven- tory) differ for these studies, contributing to an unknown extent to differences between their results. This should be kept in mind when comparing results; we therefore focused mainly on comparing impacts per 1000 kg of milk (uncorrected milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), or FPCM) between organic and conventional farms within each of these studies. For correct comparison among studies, eutrophication, acidification, and climate change impacts from this study in Fig. 5 were recalculated with the characterisation factors used in the other studies, which, for conventional and organic production, respectively, decreased eutrophication by 0.1 and 0.1%, increased acidification by 13 and 17%, and decreased climate change by 8 and 9%.

With respect to eutrophication, the German and Dutch studies found lower values for organic than for conventional milk, whereas the Swedish study found a higher value for organic milk; our study found a non-significantly lower value for organic milk. For acidifi- cation, the German, Swedish and Dutch studies found somewhat higher values for organic than for conventional milk, whereas our study found a non-significantly lower value for organic milk compared to conventional. For climate change, our study, like the

German and Swedish studies, found similar values for the two production modes; the Dutch study found somewhat higher values for organic than for conventional milk. For energy use the German, Swedish and Dutch studies found lower values for organic than for conventional milk, while our study found a non-significantly lower value for organic production. Land occupation was not evaluated in the German study; like the Swedish and Dutch studies, our study found higher values for organic than for conventional milk. While keeping in mind that the studies' methods of calculation may have contributed to differences in results, it can be noted that for eutrophication, climate change, energy use and land occupa- tion the results from our study are situated in the centre of the range of values from the studies reviewed here (Fig. 5). For acidification our results are at the lower end of the observed variability.

44: One purpose of methods for the environmental evaluation of farming systems is to identify the environmentally least harmful option amongst several alternatives. The ability of a method to do this may be compromised in many ways, two of which are partic- ularly common: (i) the analysis may give the wrong answer; and (ii) the analysis may be impossible to conduct (Hertwich et al., 1997). This raises two questions: (i) is the method valid; and (ii) is it practically feasible? Methods using means-based indicators are easy to implement but do not allow an actual evaluation of envi- ronmental impact and thus may give the wrong answer (Hansen, 1996). Methods such as LCA, using effect-based indicators, allow a better evaluation of environmental impact, but may be difficult to conduct because they are complicated, time-consuming or require data that are not available (van der Werf and Petit, 2002). EDEN-E, presented in this paper, is an attempt to develop a method combining validity and feasibility by implementing LCA in an operational tool. We feel that it represents a significant step forward in the quest for the ideal tool combining high levels of validity and user-friendliness.

Regarding EDEN-E's validity two main arguments can be put forward: (i) its conceptual framework; and (ii) the methods used to establish its emissions inventory. Its conceptual framework is based on LCA, a now widely accepted method for the environmental evaluation of industrial and agricultural systems. Its emissions inventory is largely based on internationally accepted methods and its output has been partially validated, in particular for emissions of nitrogenous substances (Payraudeau et al., 2006). This operation revealed that the tool's data collection module was sufficiently versatile to accommodate a wide variety of situations encountered on the farms and that 3-4 h of on- farm data collection were sufficient, provided basic source docu- ments were available.

The results presented in this paper will be useful as benchmark values to be used in further studies of dairy production systems in France. This study also documented the relative importance of the direct and indirect components to overall impacts, revealing the predominance of the direct component for all impacts except non- renewable energy use for the set of farms studied. Relative to previous LCA studies of cow-milk production our study yielded similar results for eutrophication, climate change, energy use and
land occupation, whereas our results were at the lower end of the observed variability for acidification.

The most valuable contribution of this study to the environ- mental analysis of farming systems lies in its exploration of the variability of farm environmental impacts. Whereas many LCA studies of agricultural production systems have examined the effect of production mode (often organic VS conventional) on environmental impacts, we have not found any papers exploring inter-farm variability within an LCA framework. This study confirms previous findings in respect to the effect of production mode on impacts, organic farms tending to have less impact than conventional farms, in particular per ha of land occupied. However, it strikingly reveals that, for the set of farms examined, the contribution of production mode to overall inter-farm variability of

This strong inter-farm variability, coupled with the small sample size for organic farms (6), may have rendered non-significant several large (>75%) differences between means by production mode. The mapping of impact variability through EDEN-E opens promising perspectives to identify ways to move towards more sustainable farming systems by identifying the structural and management character- istics of the farms presenting the lowest impacts. 52: EDEN-E represents a significant step forward in the quest for a tool for the environmental evaluation of farming systems combining high levels of validity and user-friendliness. The method allows the identification of the environmental hotspots of dairy farms, and thus helps to define priorities for environmental management. It distinguishes direct (on-farm) impacts from indi- rect impacts (associated with inputs); this distinction helps to identify improvement strategies for the farm and allows the use of

site-specific characterisation factors, in particular for direct envi- ronmental interventions contributing to regional impacts. EDEN-E allows environmental evaluation of a farm with a data collection time of 3-4 h. Thus it can be used to assess a large number of farms at a reasonable cost and constitutes a valuable tool for the exploration of inter-farm variability of environmental impacts. This study is consistent with previous studies indicating that organic farms have lower potential environmental impacts than conventional farms per ha of land occupied, but, unlike some of these studies, it did not find significant differences in impacts per 1000 kg of milk, except for land occupation. However, it strikingly reveals that for the 47 farms examined, the contribution of production mode to overall inter-farm variability is modest. 54: EDEN-E represents a significant step forward in the quest for a tool for the environmental evaluation of farming systems combining high levels of validity and user-friendliness. The method allows the identification of the environmental hotspots of dairy farms, and thus helps to define priorities for environmental management. It distinguishes direct (on-farm) impacts from indi- rect impacts (associated with inputs); this distinction helps to identify improvement strategies for the farm and allows the use of

site-specific characterisation factors, in particular for direct envi- ronmental interventions contributing to regional impacts. EDEN-E allows environmental evaluation of a farm with a data collection time of 3-4 h. Thus it can be used to assess a large number of farms at a reasonable cost and constitutes a valuable tool for the exploration of inter-farm variability of environmental impacts. This study is consistent with previous studies indicating that organic farms have lower potential environmental impacts than conventional farms per ha of land occupied, but, unlike some of these studies, it did not find significant differences in impacts per 1000 kg of milk, except for land occupation. However, it strikingly reveals that for the 47 farms examined, the contribution of production mode to overall inter-farm variability is modest.
